ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a class for nigiri sushi (FOODON_03311737) but no specific tamago nigiri subclass; closest_parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers nigiri sushi broadly; no tamago-specific code exists in the published FoodEx2 catalogue. Closest_parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept identified for tamago nigiri or nigiri sushi in the international release.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of tamago nigiri is 2 pieces, each approximately 30 g, totalling about 60 g, as typically served in Japanese sushi restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Tamago Nigiri — Singapore HPB Foodo Database (Food Composition)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record carries HPB provenance at confidence 0.95 for all core macros and secondary nutrients. Values preserved as supplied. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for egg sushi / tamagoyaki nigiri (NDB 35197) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this item; see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value for tamago nigiri exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 72 is derived from the published GI of white sushi rice (GI ~72, glucose reference, Atkinson et al. 2008 / University of Sydney GI Database), which constitutes the dominant carbohydrate component (~80% of carbohydrate mass) of tamago nigiri. The tamagoyaki topping contributes negligible carbohydrate to the overall GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 17.4) / 100 ≈ 12.5, where carbohydrate per 60 g serving = 29.03 × 0.60 = 17.4 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 374 mg/100 g with low potassium and low dietary fibre offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit tamago nigiri to 2 pieces per occasion, strictly avoid soy sauce dipping (which adds approximately 900–1,000 mg sodium per tablespoon), and account for cumulative sodium from other sushi items in the same meal, as the borderline sodium content (374 mg/100 g) combined with negligible potassium and fibre provides no meaningful cardiovascular offset.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI sushi rice (GI ~72) with added sugar in tamagoyaki and low dietary fibre (0.87 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should restrict tamago nigiri to 2–3 pieces per meal, pair with non-starchy vegetables or protein to blunt postprandial glucose rise, and be aware that the glycaemic load per standard 2-piece serving is approximately 12.5, driven by refined sushi rice seasoned with sugar and vinegar and further compounded by 5.6 g/100 g added sugar in the tamagoyaki.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate fat (5.96 g/100 g) with estimated saturated fat ~1.6 g/100 g and elevated sodium 374 mg/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals managing cardiovascular disease, tamago nigiri is acceptable in small portions (2 pieces) as part of a balanced meal, but sodium intake must be monitored carefully — soy sauce dipping should be avoided entirely — and the dish should not be consumed alongside other high-sodium or high-saturated-fat items in the same sitting.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tamago nigiri (玉子にぎり) is a classic Japanese sushi variety consisting of a hand-pressed oval mound of vinegared sushi rice topped with a thick slice of tamagoyaki — a sweet, layered Japanese rolled omelette made from eggs, mirin, sugar, and a small amount of soy sauce or dashi. The rice is seasoned with rice vinegar, sugar, and salt, giving it a subtly tangy, slightly sweet flavour. The tamagoyaki topping is soft, custardy, and mildly sweet, contrasting pleasantly with the firm, slightly sticky rice beneath. Tamago nigiri is one of the most recognisable and widely consumed sushi types globally, popular with children and adults alike, and is often used as a benchmark piece to evaluate a sushi chef's skill. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 190 kcal, 5.28 g protein, 5.96 g fat, and 29.03 g carbohydrate with only 0.87 g dietary fibre and 374 mg sodium. The high-GI refined rice base (GI ~72) combined with added sugars in both the rice seasoning and the tamagoyaki makes this a high-glycaemic food with a glycaemic load of approximately 12.5 per standard 2-piece serving. Clinically, it is unsuitable for unrestricted consumption by individuals with type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or rheumatoid arthritis due to its high glycaemic load, borderline sodium, and absence of anti-inflammatory nutrients. It is vegetarian but not vegan or halal due to egg content and potential soy sauce ingredients.
common_user_queries: How many calories in tamago nigiri?
Is tamago nigiri suitable for diabetics?
Can I eat tamago nigiri if I have high blood pressure?
How much sodium is in tamago nigiri?
What is the GI of sushi rice?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database at confidence 0.95 for all seven reported nutrients. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central closest analogue (tamagoyaki / egg sushi) and documented. GI derived from University of Sydney GI Database analogous value for white sushi rice, which is the dominant carbohydrate source.
notes: Saturated fat (1.6 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for egg sushi / tamagoyaki nigiri as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this item; this is an adapted value and should be replaced with a direct HPB or ICMR-NIN measurement when available. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 because detailed micronutrient and fatty acid fractionation data for tamago nigiri are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central for this composite dish as of 2026. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on white sushi rice (University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008); no direct GI measurement for tamago nigiri exists in the approved GI source catalogue. Halal status is not confirmed as tamagoyaki preparation may include mirin (alcohol-containing rice wine) and soy sauce formulations vary.
